text: infinity at the same time. Plato, the founder of Western epistemology, somehow saw this implosion of nothingness as a lurking threat to the ordered world around him. But in that recognition Plato makes himself a theorist of the contemporary. It is this historical development that one can attempt to capture, using van Gennep’s framework of analysis; and in spatial terms the process can best be described as a centralization of liminality. In fact, the very passing from one zone to the other has in different cultural contexts been accompanied by specific rituals, as moving in and out of such spaces could be both dangerous and contaminating, so therefore had to be controlled, constrained and ritualized. In many cases, one would need a special permit to pass though a neutral zone. In Europe, as discussed by van Gennep, this permit was called a ‘letter of marque’. Letters of marque originally constituted a license from a sovereign authorizing a subject to seek reprisals against subjects of a hostile state. It is from these observations that van Gennep can pinpoint the larger purpose of his book, Rites of Passage, and to some extent of his entire academic project: Whoever passes from one [zone] to the other finds himself physically and magico-religiously in a special situation for a certain length of time: he wavers between two worlds. It is this situation which I have designated a transition, and one of the purposes of this book is to demonstrate that this symbolic and spatial area of transition may be found in more or less pronounced form in all of the Into Liminality 13 ceremonies which accompany the passage from one social and magico-religious position to another. (van Gennep 1960: 18) From this re-statement of purpose, van Gennep moves on with rich descriptions of territorial passages in a variety of cultural contexts, starting with ancient Greece, and with the emblematic example of rituals carried out when a Spartan king went to war. Van Gennep then stresses that such procedures apply not only with reference to countries, but also in reference to a village, a town, a section of a town, a temple, or a house: ‘The neutral zone shrinks progressively till it ceases to exist except as a simple stone, a beam, or a threshold’ (ibid.: 19). The sacrality of space is written into these figurations, and rests upon a very basic recognition of boundaries, of division of spaces, of taboos surrounding and protecting the sacrality of places and boundaries, and of the necessary existence of in-between spaces or neutral zones that in all cultures are duly ritualized and venerated. Hence the near-universality of rituals pertaining to the door. The door is the boundary between the foreign and domestic worlds in the case of an ordinary dwelling, between the profane and sacred worlds in the case of a temple (ibid.: 20). Janus, the two-faced Roman god of warfare, was also deity of doors and thresholds. To cross the threshold is to unite oneself with another world. As simple as it may sound, meaningful human experience of social environments is simply not possible without such discernment. Boundaries are necessary for the framing of human experience and for thought itself. We learn to relate to the world like this; and this was captured very well by Bachelard (1994) in his Poetics of Space. Passing through a door – how irrelevantly banal! For van Gennep that one concrete act comes to stand as a condensed expression of human life itself. Doors are the passage-ways. Rites carried out on the threshold, says van Gennep, are transition rites. Rituals of purification (cleansing, washing) are rites of separation from previous surroundings; then follows rites of incorporation (often in the form of a shared meal): ‘The rites of the threshold are therefore not ‘union’ ceremonies, properly speaking, but rites of preparation for union, themselves preceded by rites of preparation for the transitional stage’. And here follows van Gennep’s refinement of his initial distinction of the three-dimensional nature of ritual passages: ‘I propose to call the rites of separation from a previous world, preliminal rites, those executed during the transitional stage liminal (or threshold) rites, and the ceremonies of incorporation into the new world post-liminal rites’. (1960: 23). Hence, to think with liminality very basically has to do with a thorough understanding of passages and passage experiences. Rooted in the Latin patio (to suffer, to undergo), the word ‘passage’ indicates a displacement, a process of transformation undertaken, but not yet finished. It designates equally ‘the site where this process, its mark or its support are enacted whatever its morphological or metaphorical sense’ (La Soudière 2000: 5). Hence, a passage is at its heart Liminality and the Modern 14 related to a certain kind of experience that one is undergoing, a movement from one position to another. Permanent Liminality: the Temporal Fixation of Liminal Conditions The spatial ‘stretching’ of liminality and the centralization of liminality here hinted to is accompanied by a temporal process, whereby liminality – in some cases, and under certain circumstances – takes on a more permanent form. Arpad Szakolczai (2000) has in this regard famously diagnosed modernity itself as a particular form of ‘permanent liminality’. In many ways, this book is an attempt to discuss, elaborate and exemplify this process of ‘permanentization’, standing in close proximity to what Weber termed ‘routinization’. The central point here seems to be the following: with regard to out-of-limit experiences, far from evaporating and being pushed to the margins, as assumed both by modernist theories of evolution, and by postmodern celebrations of the playful as an antidote to instrumentalist reason, something very different happened from the sixteenth century onwards: liminality became established at the core of the modern project. Play, comedy, gambling, sexuality, entertainment, violence – in short, all the most evident aspects of liminality linked to human experience – took central stage within cultural, political and economic modernity.
